title: Unit 6 Checkpoint Quiz — The Imperfect Tense

questions.prompt: What are the THREE irregular verbs in the imperfect?
questions.explanation: Only ser, ir, and ver are irregular in the imperfect. All other verbs — including tener, hacer, and estar — follow regular patterns.

questions.prompt: What is the nosotros imperfect of 'ir'?
questions.explanation: The nosotros imperfect of 'ir' is 'íbamos' (accent on the í). Note the difference from the preterite 'fuimos' (we went, completed trip).

questions.prompt: Which sentence uses the imperfect correctly?
options: De niña, siempre comía tacos los domingos.
De niña, siempre comí tacos los domingos.
De niña, siempre como tacos los domingos.
De niña, siempre comerá tacos los domingos.
questions.explanation: 'Siempre' + childhood habit = imperfect. 'Comía' (imperfect of comer) is correct here.

questions.prompt: What is the yo imperfect of 'ser'?
questions.explanation: The yo imperfect of 'ser' is 'era'. It looks different from 'fui' (yo preterite) and 'soy' (present). 'Era' = I was / I used to be.

questions.prompt: Which use of the imperfect is correct?
options: Setting background scenes: Hacía frío y nevaba.
Describing a sudden event: De repente hacía frío.
Describing a one-time event: Ayer hacía a las tiendas.
questions.explanation: The imperfect is perfect for background descriptions and weather conditions in a past scene. 'Hacía frío y nevaba' sets the stage.

questions.prompt: How do you say 'My grandparents had a big house'?
options: Mis abuelos tenían una casa grande.
Mis abuelos tuvieron una casa grande.
Mis abuelos tienen una casa grande.
Mis abuelos tendrán una casa grande.
questions.explanation: An ongoing past state (having a house) uses the imperfect: tenían. The preterite 'tuvieron' would imply they had it for a specific, completed period.

questions.prompt: What is the ellos imperfect of 'comer'?
questions.explanation: The ellos imperfect of -er verbs ends in -ían: comían. Don't confuse with the preterite 'comieron'.

questions.prompt: In Mexican culture, who is 'el paletero'?
options: A street vendor who sells ice cream and popsicles
questions.explanation: El paletero is an iconic figure in Mexican neighborhoods — a vendor who pushes a cart selling paletas (popsicles) and ice cream, often heard before seen.
